newHtml:
"They ask you about the soul" — the non-answer Logical Inconsistency Moderate Quran 17:85
"And they ask you, [O Muhammad], about the soul. Say, 'The soul is of the affair [i.e., concern] of my Lord. And you [i.e., mankind] have not been given of knowledge except a little.'"

What the verse says

When asked about the nature of the soul, Muhammad is instructed to reply that the soul is Allah's concern and that humans have very little knowledge.

Why this is a problem

The Quran elsewhere describes embryology, cosmology, the history of past peoples, and even specific geopolitical predictions (30:2–4). The soul — a central question of any religion — is precisely where it refuses to speak. The pattern is consistent with someone who does not know the answer, not with a divine author who chooses to withhold it: the verse does not say "I could explain but you would not grasp it" — it says "that is my Lord's concern." The question was never revisited; no later verse provides the withheld answer.

Why it fails

The verse does not address this response to children — it was delivered to the scholars of Medina who specifically asked. And the Quran does not say "you could not understand"; it says "that is my Lord's concern" — a refusal to engage, not a concession of limitations on the audience's part.

"Heavens and earth were a joined entity" — the claimed Big Bang miracle Science Claims Moderate Quran 21:30
"Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, and We separated them and made from water every living thing? Then will they not believe?"

What the verse says

Modern apologists cite this as a Quranic prediction of the Big Bang: the primordial universe was unified, then separated. The "water" clause is sometimes cited as anticipating biological origins.

Why this is a problem

The verse describes the heavens (plural) and the earth — not a primordial singularity. In the Quran's consistent cosmology, "the heavens" means the seven-heaven structure above the earth. "Joined then separated" is the standard Near Eastern creation picture of sky being lifted off earth, found in the Babylonian Enuma Elish, Genesis 1:6–9, and Sumerian literature. The Big Bang is the expansion of spacetime itself from a singularity 13.8 billion years ago, long before Earth existed. The two accounts cannot be mapped onto each other without distorting both.

Why it fails

The test of such a claim is whether the Quran predicts something in advance of discovery. No such prediction exists. Every Quranic "scientific miracle" is identified after the science settles, never before — the pattern of compatibility-after-the-fact, not prediction.

A day with Allah is 1,000 years — or 50,000 years Contradiction Moderate Quran 22:47, 32:5 vs 70:4
"...indeed, a day with your Lord is like a thousand years of those which you count." (22:47)
"The angels and the Spirit will ascend to Him during a Day the extent of which is fifty thousand years." (70:4)

What the verses say

Two verses state a divine day equals a thousand human years. A third (70:4) states it equals fifty thousand. All three use the same grammatical construction addressing divine temporal scale.

Why this is a problem

This is a straightforward numerical contradiction. The factor of fifty is not rounding error — it is the difference between two distinct factual claims about Allah's temporal scale. The Quran in 4:82 invites readers to test it for contradictions; a literal thousand versus a literal fifty thousand years within the same thematic category is precisely the kind of discrepancy that test should catch.

Why it fails

The "different events" reading is rescue logic: the texts do not supply the distinction, and importing different referents for similar-looking verses is a technique that can dissolve any contradiction in any scripture. If a book can never be shown to contradict itself because every apparent contradiction is rescued by hypothesizing different referents, its internal consistency claim becomes unfalsifiable and therefore informationally empty.

One hundred lashes for fornication — yet the hadith demands stoning Contradiction Women Moderate Quran 24:2 vs hadith (Bukhari 6829, Muslim 1691)
"The [unmarried] woman or [unmarried] man found guilty of sexual intercourse — lash each one of them with a hundred lashes..."

What the verse says

The Quran prescribes 100 lashes for fornication (zina). Note: the Saheeh translation inserts "[unmarried]" in brackets, but the Arabic text simply says "the fornicator" — the marital distinction is added by translators to pre-empt a doctrinal problem.

Why this is a problem

Classical Islamic law punishes adultery by stoning to death, grounded in hadith where Muhammad personally ordered stonings (Bukhari 6829, Muslim 1691). A hadith attributed to Umar claims the stoning verse was once in the Quran but was abrogated in text while remaining binding in law. This creates a stark dilemma: if the Quran is complete and clear (5:3, 6:38), why does Islamic criminal law require hadith to complete it? If the stoning verse was truly removed from the Quran, the doctrine of perfect Quranic preservation (15:9) is undermined.

Why it fails

Accepting the hadith-sourced distinction concedes that the Quran is not a self-sufficient legal source — it requires hadith to complete it. And the claim that the stoning verse was removed from the Quran while remaining legally binding concedes that what we have is not a perfectly preserved text. Both concessions damage the Quran's doctrinal self-description.

The talking ant warning the colony about Solomon's army Strange / Obscure Moderate Quran 27:18–19
"Until, when they came upon the valley of the ants, an ant said, 'O ants, enter your dwellings that you not be crushed by Solomon and his soldiers while they perceive not.' So [Solomon] smiled, amused at her speech..."

What the verse says

A female ant uses structured propositional speech to warn a colony by name about Solomon's approaching army. Solomon — who in the Quran understands animal speech — overhears and smiles at her words.

Why this is a problem

Ants communicate through pheromones, tactile signals, and stridulation — not propositional language. The ant in this verse identifies Solomon by name, reasons abstractly ("so you will not be crushed"), and formulates a plan. This is not animal communication; it is talking-animal folklore. The entire Solomon cycle in Surah 27 — commanding jinn, riding the wind, talking animals, the hoopoe bird reporting on a distant queen — parallels Jewish midrashic literature (the Targum Sheni on Esther, the Testament of Solomon) that circulated in pre-Islamic Arabia. The Quran reproduces late-antique Jewish legend as divine history.

Why it fails

The specific claim is that the ant used structured speech intelligible to a human king, and that the king was amused at the articulateness of her words. This is not a report of chemical communication — it is a talking-animal folktale, and its direct parallels in Jewish midrashic literature pre-dating the Quran identify its origin.

"Only Allah knows what's in the wombs" — ultrasound says otherwise Science Claims Moderate Quran 31:34
"Indeed, Allah [alone] has knowledge of the Hour and sends down the rain and knows what is in the wombs..."

What the verse says

Five things are listed as known only to Allah, including what is in the wombs and when it will rain.

Why this is a problem

Two of the five are now routinely known by humans. Ultrasound reveals sex, anatomy, developmental stage, and genetic conditions. Amniocentesis provides full karyotype. Meteorological forecasting predicts rainfall days in advance with useful accuracy. The Quran's claim is not "Allah knows these best" — the Arabic construction implies exclusive possession of the knowledge. Technology has falsified that exclusivity on at least two of the five items.

Why it fails

This narrowing is a post-hoc move to avoid refutation: the 7th-century audience hearing "what is in the wombs" would have understood sex, health, and survival. The hadith corpus reads the verse in this natural sense (Bukhari 50). Restricting it to the soul's eternal destiny is exactly the kind of move made every time science catches up to a Quranic claim — a pattern of compatibility-after-the-fact, not of consistent prior interpretation.

A worm eats Solomon's staff — and only then do the jinn notice he is dead Strange / Obscure Moderate Quran 34:14
"And when We decreed for him [i.e., Solomon] death, nothing indicated to them [i.e., the jinn] his death except a creature of the earth eating his staff. But when he fell, it became clear to the jinn that if they had known the unseen, they would not have remained in humiliating punishment."

What the verse says

Solomon dies while leaning on his staff. The jinn — enslaved to his construction projects — continue working around his corpse until a worm eats through the staff and Solomon's body collapses, revealing he has been dead for some time.

Why this is a problem

A corpse leaning on a staff does not remain upright for hours, let alone long enough for a worm to eat through wood — rigor mortis, decomposition, and simple balance make this impossible. The verse is drawn from the same legendary stock as the rest of the Quranic Solomon cycle (Targum Sheni, Testament of Solomon). It is presented as a theological point about jinn ignorance of the unseen, but the specific physics fail: any audience in the real world would have noticed the king was dead long before a worm was needed.

Why it fails

This miracle is not mentioned in the verse. Adding it to make the story work concedes that the narrative requires miraculous intervention not present in the text to be physically coherent. A text claiming divine authorship should not require centuries of commentary to insert the physics that make it viable. The story functions as fable, not physical description.

"We have made it an Arabic Quran" — why would God prefer a language? Logical Inconsistency Moderate Quran 12:2, 43:3
"Indeed, We have sent it down as an Arabic Quran that you might understand." (12:2)

What the verses say

The Quran repeatedly emphasizes its Arabic character as an intentional divine choice — so the Arabic-speaking audience of Mecca and Medina would understand. This appears across at least six passages.

Why this is a problem

The Arabic-lock creates a deep tension with Islam's universalist claim. Roughly 75% of today's Muslims are non-native Arabic speakers, yet classical Islam holds that translations of the Quran are not the Quran — only Arabic is Allah's word. This means most Muslims have never read the Quran; they have read interpretations. The traditional proof of divine origin — the literary inimitability (i'jaz) of the Quran — is inaccessible to most humans who have ever lived. A proof available only to Arabic readers is a proof only for Arabic readers, an odd design choice for a God who claims to have sent a message to all of humanity.

Why it fails

Either Muhammad is one in a sequence of localized prophets — in which case other nations should have their own contemporary prophets — or he is universal, in which case the Arabic-lock undermines universality. The two positions cannot both be comprehensively maintained.

"All things We created with predestination" — then punishment becomes incoherent Logical Inconsistency Moderate Quran 54:49 (also 57:22, 76:30)
"Indeed, all things We created with predestination." (54:49)

What the verses say

Everything that happens is pre-decreed, written before creation (57:22), and created by Allah with predestination. This is the doctrine of qadar — foundational to Sunni belief.

Why this is a problem

The Quran simultaneously holds that every event including every human choice is pre-written (54:49, 57:22) and that humans will be judged eternally for those choices (3:30, 99:7–8). The classical solutions — Mu'tazilite free will (condemned as heresy), Ash'arite kasb (humans "acquire" acts Allah creates) — either contradict the predestination verses or are verbal formulations that describe the problem without solving it. If Allah creates the act, the human does not originate it; if the human does not originate it, they cannot be justly punished for it. The Quran itself does not offer a resolution — it asserts both without a mechanism connecting them.

Why it fails

The Quran says Allah created all things with predestination (54:49) and wrote them in a register before they happened (57:22). Mere foreknowledge would not be problematic; creation-plus-predestination is. "We cannot understand the mystery" is honest but is not a resolution — it is the label for a failure of coherence.

"Able even to proportion his fingertips" — the fingerprint miracle claim Science Claims Moderate Quran 75:3–4
"Does man think that We will not assemble his bones? Yes. [We are] Able [even] to proportion his fingertips."

What the verse says

In a passage about resurrection, Allah emphasizes His power to reassemble not only bones but the smallest detail — the fingertips. Modern apologists claim this predicted the uniqueness of fingerprints, a discovery of 19th-century forensic science.

Why this is a problem

The verse mentions fingertips (banan) and uses the verb meaning to shape or proportion — referring to Allah's precise reconstructive power, not to forensic identification. No pre-modern Muslim commentator (Tabari, Ibn Kathir, al-Razi, al-Qurtubi) read this verse as being about fingerprints. The "fingerprint miracle" is a 20th-century reading invented after the science was already known. No one reading the text before 1858 (the year fingerprint uniqueness was scientifically established) would have extracted the claim — which is the test of genuine prediction. A verse that is retroactively compatible with a discovery is not predicting it.

Why it fails

Compatibility is not prediction. A text loose enough to be compatible with any future finding will always appear to have anticipated it in hindsight. Fourteen centuries of tafsir produced zero scholars who extracted the fingerprint reading — which is what we would expect if the verse does not actually contain it.

"And at the earth — how it is spread out" — a flat-earth picture Science Claims Moderate Quran 88:20 (also 2:22, 51:48, 71:19)
"And at the earth — how it is spread out?" (88:20)
"[He] who made for you the earth a bed [spread out] and the sky a ceiling..." (2:22)

What the verses say

Multiple Quranic passages describe the earth using verbs and images of spreading, flattening, and laying out as a bed or carpet. The sky is described as a ceiling raised above it. Mountains peg the earth down. This cosmological picture recurs across nine or more passages.

Why this is a problem

The earth is an oblate spheroid. The composite Quranic image — flat earth spread below a raised sky-ceiling, held down by mountain-pegs — is the standard ancient Near Eastern flat-earth picture found in Genesis, Babylonian creation texts, and pre-Socratic Greek thought. Medieval Muslim astronomers knew the earth was spherical from Aristotle and Ptolemy, not from the Quran — and some conservative scholars (Ibn Taymiyya) read the spreading language as literal, precisely because it is.

Why it fails

This is a post-hoc reading the original audience would not have made. It trades the scientific-miracle claim (Quran anticipated spherical geography) for a neutral claim (Quran is compatible with spherical geography). A text that accommodates any cosmology is not predicting one — it is ambiguous.

"The lying, sinning forelock" — the frontal-lobe miracle claim Science Claims Moderate Quran 96:15–16
"No! If he does not desist, We will surely drag him by the forelock — a lying, sinning forelock."

What the verse says

A curse against an opponent (traditionally Abu Jahl): Allah threatens to drag him by his forelock, calling it "lying" and "sinning." Modern apologetics (Zakir Naik, Harun Yahya, the Keith Moore Quran supplement) cites this as a prediction of frontal-lobe neuroscience — since the prefrontal cortex, located behind the forehead, governs decision-making and behavior control.

Why this is a problem

The verse is moral invective — calling an enemy's forelock (a standard Arabic metonym for a proud man) "lying and sinning" in a threat. It is not an anatomical claim. No classical commentator read it as brain science. The frontal-lobe reading was invented in the late 20th century after the relevant neuroscience was already established. More precisely: behavioral control is distributed across the prefrontal cortex, limbic system, anterior cingulate, and other structures — the verse's implicit claim of a single lying-organ in the forelock region is an oversimplification even by modern standards.

Why it fails

The principled test: does the text, read naturally and historically, contain specific anatomical claims? The answer is no — it contains moral invective using a body metaphor. Fourteen centuries of tafsir contain zero scholars who extracted an anatomical reading before modern neuroscience prompted the search.
